name: Iron Smelting (Bloomery Tradition)
tagline: Reducing iron ore in a solid-state bloomery to produce wrought iron for tools and weapons
problem:
Bronze is excellent but constrained by tin availability and cost gradients. Iron oxide is extraordinarily common — banded formations, bog ores, laterites — promising a metal for populations far from tin trade routes. The problem is thermodynamics and kinetics: iron melts far hotter than copper under simple charcoal furnaces, so early ironmaking produces a spongy solid bloom of iron and slag rather than a clean pourable puddle like bronze.

Separating iron metal from glassy slag inclusions requires prolonged hammering and folding — labour-intensive but doable at village scale. Controlling carbon uptake distinguishes soft wrought iron from harder steely regions within the same bloom.

Iron also changes warfare and agriculture: ploughshares, axes, and nails scale with ore proximity. The technology is not 'better bronze' — it is a different process chain with different failure modes (corrosion, brittleness if carburised wrong).
overview:
A bloomery furnace stacks charcoal and iron ore with forced air through a tuyère. Carbon monoxide reduces hematite (Fe2O3) or magnetite (Fe3O4) to metallic iron in the solid state, while silica-rich gangue melts into slag that trickles downward and is tapped or drains. Operating temperature stays below the melting point of pure iron under these conditions, so metal accumulates as a pasty mass — the bloom.

The smelt ends when the operator judges from flame colour, slag viscosity, and time. The bloom is extracted hot and compacted under hammering, expelling liquid slag and welding iron fibres together. Repeated heats and folds align inclusions and homogenise composition somewhat.

Ore preparation matters: roasting drives off hydrate water and converts carbonates; sizing ore pieces balances reaction rate against furnace permeability. Too fine chokes airflow; too coarse under-reacts.

This entry describes the bloomery lineage — ancestor to later finery processes and, in a different branch, blast furnaces producing pig iron. The Codex separates those because the tooling, fuels, and products diverge strongly.

principles.name: Solid-state reduction
principles.explanation: Below iron's melting point, oxide grains are reduced outward-in; metallic iron sinters into a porous bloom while slag percolates through pores.

principles.name: Carburisation from charcoal contact
principles.explanation: Iron can dissolve carbon from the coke bed, locally raising hardness — controlled in later steelmaking, often incidental in bloomery output.

principles.name: Slag basicity
principles.explanation: Adding silica versus lime-bearing flux changes slag fluidity and sulphur partitioning — even bloomery operators learned empirical flux recipes.

principles.name: Work consolidation
principles.explanation: Hammering raises temperature via deformation work and squeezes out slag — mechanical finishing is part of smelting, not optional.

components.name: Bloomery shaft
components.function: Contains stacked charcoal and ore; focuses reaction zone
components.position: Slag tapping hole near base; tuyère enters above hearth
components.criticalNote: Tuyère can melt back — water-cooled copper tuyères appear historically in advanced setups

components.name: Tuyère
components.position: Penetrates furnace wall toward hearth centreline
components.criticalNote: Blast rate sets peak temperature — too high burns iron; too low fails to reduce

components.name: Bellows
components.position: Human or water-powered in later mills
components.criticalNote: Consistent rhythm matters more than peak puff for many furnaces

components.name: Consolidation hammer
components.madeFrom: Stone maul early; iron hammer later
components.criticalNote: Hot short cracks if worked too cold — keep yellow heat

components.name: Use-case boundary
components.function: Defines what Iron Smelting (Bloomery Tradition) is meant to accomplish and what it must not be used for
components.madeFrom: Written requirements, hazard notes, local constraints, and responsible operator assignment
components.criticalNote: A vague use case leads to unsafe improvisation.

rawMaterials.name: Iron ore
earthLocations: Goa and Bellary in India historically
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Martian regolith contains iron oxides reducible with CO/H2 at high temperature
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Roasting, crushing to walnut size typical

rawMaterials.name: Charcoal
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Electrowinning supersedes when power is cheap

buildSteps.title: Prepare ore and charcoal
buildSteps.description: Roast carbonates and hydrates; crush to consistent size. Charcoal should be metallurgical grade — rings when struck, few fines.
buildSteps.warningNote: Silica dust from crushing — respiratory hazard

buildSteps.title: Dry out and preheat furnace
buildSteps.description: Slow wood fire first; convert to charcoal bed. Seal cracks with clay slurry.

buildSteps.title: Run the smelt
buildSteps.description: Layer charcoal and ore while maintaining blast. Tap slag when it appears at eye port. Record time — learning curve is steep.
buildSteps.warningNote: CO exposure — work upwind; ventilated apron not optional ethically

buildSteps.title: Extract bloom
buildSteps.description: Break furnace top or pull bloom through front. Move to anvil while hot enough to weld under blows.
buildSteps.warningNote: Slag spray from hammering — face shield

buildSteps.title: Consolidate and split samples
buildSteps.description: Forge fold to drive out glassy stringers. Break test bars to check toughness versus brittleness.
buildSteps.warningNote: High-carbon regions may shatter — normalise by extended soak if you have controlled hearth later

history.event: Iron artefacts increase in the Near East; Hittite associations and trade in iron goods appear in texts and archaeology.
Iron tools become dominant across Eurasian agriculture and warfare, though bronze persists for cast vessels.

impact:
Iron decoupled metal supply from tin networks for many societies, though charcoal demand reshaped forests. It enabled cheaper cutting edges and nails — mundane objects that scale construction.

Technically, bloomery iron is a bridge to fining, puddling, and Bessemer steel. Understanding that ironmaking is a family of processes prevents cargo-cult rebuilding of the wrong furnace for available ore.

sources.title: OSHA — Heat illness prevention
sources.note: Thermal stress and industrial hygiene for furnace work

sources.title: U.S. EPA — Sustainability
sources.note: Environmental management framing for emissions and waste from smelting
